title: Intersection of two arrays
description: Find common elements between two arrays

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: Take first element from arr1: 1. Check arr2: 3 (no match), 4 (no match), 5 (no match), 6 (no match). No match found. Step 3: Take second element from arr1: 2. Check arr2: 3 (no match), 4 (no match), 5 (no match), 6 (no match). No match found. Step 4: Take third element from arr1: 3. Check arr2: 3 (match found, 3 not in result). Add 3 to result: result = [3]. Step 5: Take fourth element from arr1: 4. Check arr2: 3 (no match), 4 (match found, 4 not in result). Add 4 to result: result = [3, 4]. Step 6: Return result = [3, 4].

Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: Take first element from arr1: 1. Check arr2: 2 (no match), 2 (no match). No match found. Step 3: Take second element from arr1: 2. Check arr2: 2 (match found, 2 not in result). Add 2 to result: result = [2]. Step 4: Take third element from arr1: 2. Check arr2: 2 (match found, but 2 already in result). Skip adding duplicate. Step 5: Take fourth element from arr1: 1. Check arr2: 2 (no match), 2 (no match). No match found. Step 6: Return result = [2].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each element in the first array and check if it exists in the second array by scanning it entirely. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if output is not counted, else O(k) for result storage

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Intersection of Two Arrays
ladders.desc: Find all common elements between two arrays using nested iteration.

explanation.prose: The brute force approach to finding the intersection of two arrays is conceptually simple but computationally expensive for large inputs. The intuition is straightforward: for each element in the first array, we check if it exists anywhere in the second array by scanning through all elements of the second array. This works because we're exhaustively comparing every possible pair of elements between the two arrays. The algorithm proceeds as follows: 1) Initialize an empty result list to store common elements. 2) Iterate through each element in array1. 3) For each element in array1, iterate through all elements in array2 to check for a match. 4) If a match is found and the element isn't already in the result (to handle duplicates), add it to the result. 5) Return the result list. The The This approach connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the element search operation.

timeComplexity.note: where n and m are the lengths of the two arrays, as we have nested loops

spaceComplexity.note: where k is the number of common elements, or O(1) if we don't count the output storage

commonMistakes: not handling duplicates properly (adding the same element multiple times)
not considering empty arrays
incorrectly assuming arrays are sorted. The brute force method is inefficient but serves as a baseline for understanding more optimized approaches

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: Take first element from arr1: 1. Check arr2: 3 (no match), 4 (no match), 5 (no match), 6 (no match). No match found. Step 3: Take second element from arr1: 2. Check arr2: 3 (no match), 4 (no match), 5 (no match), 6 (no match). No match found. Step 4: Take third element from arr1: 3. Check arr2: 3 (match found, 3 not in result). Add 3 to result: result = [3]. Step 5: Take fourth element from arr1: 4. Check arr2: 3 (no match), 4 (match found, 4 not in result). Add 4 to result: result = [3, 4]. Step 6: Return result = [3, 4].

Step 1: Initialize result = []. Step 2: Take first element from arr1: 1. Check arr2: 2 (no match), 2 (no match). No match found. Step 3: Take second element from arr1: 2. Check arr2: 2 (match found, 2 not in result). Add 2 to result: result = [2]. Step 4: Take third element from arr1: 2. Check arr2: 2 (match found, but 2 already in result). Skip adding duplicate. Step 5: Take fourth element from arr1: 1. Check arr2: 2 (no match), 2 (no match). No match found. Step 6: Return result = [2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Search in Array
ladders.desc: Check if a target element exists in an array by scanning through all elements.

explanation.prose: The linear search algorithm is the fundamental operation at the heart of the brute force intersection approach. The intuition is simple: to determine if a specific element exists in an array, we examine each element one by one until we either find the target or exhaust all possibilities. This works because arrays provide sequential access to elements, allowing us to traverse them in order. The algorithm: 1) Start from the first element of the array. 2) Compare the current element with the target. 3) If they match, return True (or the index). 4) If not, move to the next element. 5) If we reach the end without finding a match, return False (or -1). This connects to the core logic (L2) of nested iteration, where linear search becomes the inner loop.

timeComplexity.note: where n is the array length, as we may need to check every element in the worst case

spaceComplexity.note: as we only use a few variables

commonMistakes: not handling empty arrays
off-by-one errors in loop bounds
returning too early when duplicates might exist. In the intersection problem context
we use linear search to check if each element from the first array exists in the second array

examples.trace: Step 1: Check arr[0] = 3, compare with target 4: 3 ≠ 4. Step 2: Check arr[1] = 4, compare with target 4: 4 = 4. Match found. Return True.

Step 1: Check arr[0] = 1, compare with target 5: 1 ≠ 5. Step 2: Check arr[1] = 2, compare with target 5: 2 ≠ 5. Step 3: Check arr[2] = 3, compare with target 5: 3 ≠ 5. Step 4: Reached end of array. No match found. Return False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Iteration Pattern
ladders.desc: Use one loop inside another to compare all pairs of elements between two collections.

explanation.prose: Nested iteration is the algorithmic pattern that enables comparing every element from one array with every element from another array. The intuition is that to find relationships between two sets of data, we need to examine all possible combinations of elements from both sets. This works because the outer loop controls which element from the first array we're considering, while the inner loop examines all elements in the second array for that particular element. The pattern: 1) Outer loop iterates through each element in array1. 2) For each iteration of the outer loop, inner loop iterates through all elements in array2. 3) Perform comparison operation between current element from array1 and current element from array2. This connects to the building block (L3) of array traversal, as nested loops rely on sequential access to array elements.

timeComplexity.note: where n and m are the sizes of the two arrays, as the inner loop runs completely for each iteration of the outer loop

spaceComplexity.note: for the iteration itself (not counting any result storage)

commonMistakes: confusing which array is in which loop
inefficient early termination conditions
not resetting inner loop variables properly. In the intersection problem
this pattern allows us to check each element from the first array against all elements of the second array

examples.trace: Step 1: Outer loop i=0 (arr1[0]=1). Inner loop j=0: compare 1 with arr2[0]=3 (no match). Inner loop j=1: compare 1 with arr2[1]=4 (no match). Step 2: Outer loop i=1 (arr1[1]=2). Inner loop j=0: compare 2 with arr2[0]=3 (no match). Inner loop j=1: compare 2 with arr2[1]=4 (no match). Total comparisons: 2*2 = 4.
Step 1: Outer loop i=0 (arr1[0]=5). Inner loop j=0: compare 5 with arr2[0]=5 (match found). Inner loop j=1: compare 5 with arr2[1]=6 (no match). Total comparisons: 1*2 = 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Index
ladders.desc: Access each element in an array sequentially using index-based iteration.

explanation.prose: Array traversal using indices is the fundamental skill needed to implement loops that process array elements. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations with indices starting from 0, allowing us to access elements in order by incrementing an index variable. This works because arrays provide O(1) random access to any element when we know its index. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an index variable (usually i) to 0. 2) While i is less than the array length, access array[i]. 3) Perform desired operation on array[i]. 4) Increment i by 1. 5) Repeat until i equals array length. This connects to the basic operation (L4) of element access, as traversal relies on repeatedly accessing elements by index.
commonMistakes: off-by-one errors (using <= instead of < for length)
modifying the array while iterating
not handling empty arrays. In the intersection problem context
we need to traverse both arrays completely to compare all elements

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=10. Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=20. Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=30. Step 4: i=3, which equals length 3, stop.
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=5. Step 2: i=1, which equals length 1, stop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve the value stored at a specific position in an array.

explanation.prose: Accessing array elements by index is the most fundamental array operation. The intuition is that arrays are data structures that store elements in sequential memory locations, with each position identified by a numerical index (typically starting at 0). This works because the array maintains a reference to the starting memory address, and accessing any element involves calculating an offset from that starting point. The operation is simple: given an array 'arr' and an index 'i', retrieve the value at arr[i]. This connects to the concept foundation (L5) of arrays as indexed collections, as element access relies on understanding how arrays organize data.

spaceComplexity.note: as no additional memory is needed

commonMistakes: accessing indices outside the valid range (causing index errors)
confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based indexing
not understanding that arrays have fixed size. In the intersection problem
we need to access elements from both arrays repeatedly during comparison operations

examples.trace: Array has indices 0, 1, 2 with values 10, 20, 30 respectively. Accessing index 1 retrieves the value 20.
Array has indices 0, 1, 2, 3 with values 5, 6, 7, 8 respectively. Accessing index 0 retrieves the first value 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays as Indexed Collections
ladders.desc: Understand that arrays store multiple values accessible by numerical positions.

explanation.prose: Arrays are fundamental data structures that store collections of elements, each identified by a numerical index. The intuition is that instead of having separate variables for related data, we can group them together in a single array structure where position indicates which element we're referring to. This works because arrays allocate contiguous memory blocks and use indices as offsets from the starting address. Key concepts: 1) Arrays have a fixed size determined at creation. 2) Elements are stored sequentially. 3) Indexing typically starts at 0 (0-based indexing). 4) All elements are usually of the same data type. 5) Arrays provide O(1) access to any element when you know its index. Time complexity for access is O(1). This foundation connects directly to element access (L4), as understanding array structure is prerequisite to accessing elements. Common beginner mistakes include: thinking indices start at 1, not understanding that arrays have fixed size, and confusing arrays with other collection types. In the intersection problem context, we need two arrays to store the input data we want to compare.

examples.trace: The array stores three integer values. Position 0 contains 5, position 1 contains 10, and position 2 contains 15. The array length is 3.
The array stores four character values. Position 0 contains 'a', position 1 contains 'b', position 2 contains 'c', and position 3 contains 'd'. The array length is 4.

approaches.name: Sorting and Two Pointer O(n log n + m log m)
approaches.summary: Sort both arrays and use two pointers to traverse them simultaneously to find common elements. This avoids extra space beyond sorting overhead.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n + m log m)
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if sorting in-place, else O(n+m) for sorting, plus O(k) for result

ladders.title: Full Problem — Intersection of Two Arrays Using Sorting and Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find common elements between two arrays by sorting and using two pointers.

explanation.prose: The intuition behind this approach is that by sorting both arrays, we can systematically compare elements in a single pass using two pointers, avoiding the need for extra data structures like hash sets. Sorting brings similar elements together, allowing us to efficiently find matches. The algorithm first sorts both input arrays in non-decreasing order. Then, it initializes two pointers, i and j, at the start of each array. While both pointers are within bounds, compare the elements at these positions. If they are equal, add the element to the result (ensuring no duplicates by checking against the last added element if needed) and increment both pointers. If the element in the first array is smaller, increment i to move to a potentially larger element that might match. If the element in the second array is smaller, increment j. This process continues until one array is fully traversed. The This approach is efficient for medium-sized arrays and avoids extra space overhead beyond sorting.

connectsTo.reason: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of the two-pointer traversal after sorting, which is detailed in L1

timeComplexity.value: O(n log n + m log m)
timeComplexity.note: due to sorting, where n and m are the lengths of the arrays, and O(n + m) for the two-pointer traversal, but the sorting dominates

spaceComplexity.note: if sorting is done in-place, plus O(k) for the result list, where k is the number of common elements

commonMistakes: not handling duplicates correctly (e.g
adding the same element multiple times) or forgetting to sort the arrays first
which breaks the two-pointer logic. To avoid this
always sort
skip duplicates by comparing with the last added element in the result
explanation.codePattern: def intersection(nums1, nums2): nums1.sort(); nums2.sort(); i, j = 0, 0; result = []; while i < len(nums1) and j < len(nums2): if nums1[i] == nums2[j]: if not result or result[-1] != nums1[i]: result.append(nums1[i]); i += 1; j += 1; elif nums1[i] < nums2[j]: i += 1; else: j += 1; return result

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort nums1: [1, 1, 2, 2]; Sort nums2: [2, 2]. Step 2: Initialize i=0, j=0, result=[]. Step 3: nums1[0]=1, nums2[0]=2, 1<2, so i=1. Step 4: nums1[1]=1, nums2[0]=2, 1<2, so i=2. Step 5: nums1[2]=2, nums2[0]=2, equal, add 2 to result: [2], i=3, j=1. Step 6: nums1[3]=2, nums2[1]=2, equal, but result[-1]=2, skip duplicate, i=4, j=2. Step 7: i=4 (out of bounds), stop. Return [2].

Step 1: Sort nums1: [4, 5, 9]; Sort nums2: [4, 4, 8, 9, 9]. Step 2: i=0, j=0, result=[]. Step 3: nums1[0]=4, nums2[0]=4, equal, add 4: [4], i=1, j=1. Step 4: nums1[1]=5, nums2[1]=4, 5>4, so j=2. Step 5: nums1[1]=5, nums2[2]=8, 5<8, so i=2. Step 6: nums1[2]=9, nums2[2]=8, 9>8, so j=3. Step 7: nums1[2]=9, nums2[3]=9, equal, add 9: [4, 9], i=3, j=4. Step 8: i=3 (out of bounds), stop. Return [4, 9].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two-Pointer Traversal on Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: The core traversal logic to find common elements after arrays are sorted.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the heart of the full solution, where we use two pointers to efficiently scan through two sorted arrays to identify common elements. The intuition is that sorting aligns elements in order, so we can compare them systematically without missing any matches. Start with pointers i and j at the beginning of each sorted array. In each step, compare the elements at these positions. If they match, we've found a common element—add it to the result (avoiding duplicates by checking the last element in the result) and move both pointers forward to continue searching. If the element in the first array is smaller, increment i to look for a larger element that might match the current element in the second array. Conversely, if the element in the second array is smaller, increment j. This ensures we never skip potential matches because the arrays are sorted. The process stops when either pointer reaches the end of its array, as no more matches are possible.

connectsTo.reason: This sub-routine is executed after sorting in the full problem to find the intersection

timeComplexity.note: for the traversal, where n and m are array lengths, but this is overshadowed by sorting in the full problem

spaceComplexity.note: for pointers, plus O(k) for the result

commonMistakes: not handling edge cases like empty arrays or forgetting to skip duplicates
which can lead to incorrect outputs. To avoid this
always check pointer bounds
compare with the last result element.  Connection to L2: It builds on the core logic of comparing sorted elements
explanation.codePattern: def two_pointer_intersection(sorted1, sorted2): i, j = 0, 0; result = []; while i < len(sorted1) and j < len(sorted2): if sorted1[i] == sorted2[j]: if not result or result[-1] != sorted1[i]: result.append(sorted1[i]); i += 1; j += 1; elif sorted1[i] < sorted2[j]: i += 1; else: j += 1; return result

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0, result=[]. Step 2: sorted1[0]=1, sorted2[0]=2, 1<2, i=1. Step 3: sorted1[1]=2, sorted2[0]=2, equal, add 2: [2], i=2, j=1. Step 4: sorted1[2]=3, sorted2[1]=3, equal, add 3: [2, 3], i=3, j=2. Step 5: i=3 (out of bounds), stop. Return [2, 3].

Step 1: i=0, j=0, result=[]. Step 2: sorted1[0]=5, sorted2[0]=5, equal, add 5: [5], i=1, j=1. Step 3: sorted1[1]=5, sorted2[1]=6, 5<6, i=2. Step 4: sorted1[2]=6, sorted2[1]=6, equal, add 6: [5, 6], i=3, j=2. Step 5: i=3 (out of bounds), stop. Return [5, 6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing Elements in Sorted Order
ladders.desc: The technique of comparing elements from two sorted sequences to find matches.

explanation.prose: This core logic involves the fundamental idea that when two sequences are sorted, we can compare their elements in a way that efficiently identifies common values without redundant checks. The intuition is that sorting brings order, allowing us to use a greedy approach: start from the smallest elements and move forward, ensuring we never backtrack. By comparing the current elements of both sequences, if they are equal, we record a match and advance in both sequences because any future matches would involve larger elements. If one element is smaller, we advance only in that sequence, as the smaller element cannot match any remaining elements in the other sequence (which are all larger or equal). This linear scan guarantees that all common elements are found in O(n + m) time after sorting. This technique is widely used in problems involving sorted arrays, such as merging or finding intersections. Time complexity for this logic alone is O(n + m), and .

connectsTo.reason: This logic is implemented in the two-pointer traversal

commonMistakes: not advancing pointers correctly when elements are not equal
which can lead to infinite loops or missed matches. To avoid this
always advance the pointer pointing to the smaller element.  Connection to L3: It relies on the building block of sorting to enable ordered comparison
explanation.codePattern: while i < len(arr1) and j < len(arr2): if arr1[i] == arr2[j]: # handle match; i += 1; j += 1; elif arr1[i] < arr2[j]: i += 1; else: j += 1

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0: 1 vs 2, 1<2, i=1. Step 2: i=1, j=0: 3 vs 2, 3>2, j=1. Step 3: i=1, j=1: 3 vs 3, equal, record 3, i=2, j=2. Step 4: i=2, j=2: 5 vs 4, 5>4, j=3. Step 5: j=3 (out of bounds), stop. Common elements: [3].

Step 1: i=0, j=0: 2 vs 4, 2<4, i=1. Step 2: i=1, j=0: 4 vs 4, equal, record 4, i=2, j=1. Step 3: i=2, j=1: 6 vs 5, 6>5, j=2. Step 4: i=2, j=2: 6 vs 6, equal, record 6, i=3, j=3. Step 5: i=3 (out of bounds), stop. Common elements: [4, 6].

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting an Array
ladders.desc: The intermediate concept of arranging elements in non-decreasing order.

explanation.prose: Sorting is a fundamental algorithmic building block that rearranges elements of an array into a specific order, typically non-decreasing (ascending). In the context of the intersection problem, sorting transforms the arrays so that similar elements are grouped together, enabling efficient comparison via two pointers. The intuition is that order brings structure: when arrays are sorted, we can traverse them linearly without missing potential matches, as elements increase monotonically. Common sorting algorithms include quicksort, mergesort, or built-in sorts like Python's Timsort, with average time complexity O(n log n) for an array of length n. Space complexity varies: O(log n) for quicksort (in-place but recursive) or O(n) for mergesort. For this problem, we assume in-place sorting for O(1) extra space or O(n) if using auxiliary arrays. Sorting is crucial because it reduces the problem from potentially O(n*m) brute-force comparisons to O(n log n + m log m) with the two-pointer scan.

connectsTo.reason: Sorting enables the core logic of comparing elements in order

commonMistakes: not sorting both arrays or using unstable sorts that might affect order but not correctness for this problem. To avoid this
always apply a reliable sorting method.  Connection to L4: It uses basic operations like comparisons
swaps

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with [3, 1, 2]. Step 2: Compare 3 and 1: 3>1, swap to [1, 3, 2]. Step 3: Compare 3 and 2: 3>2, swap to [1, 2, 3]. Step 4: Array is now sorted: [1, 2, 3].

Step 1: Start with [5, 5, 4, 3]. Step 2: Use a sorting algorithm like quicksort: pick pivot 5, partition into [3, 4, 5, 5]. Step 3: Recursively sort left and right if needed. Step 4: Final sorted array: [3, 4, 5, 5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of determining the relationship between two numeric values.

explanation.prose: Comparing two numbers is a basic operation in programming that involves evaluating their relative values: whether one is less than, equal to, or greater than the other. In the intersection problem, this operation is used repeatedly in the two-pointer traversal to decide how to move pointers. The intuition is that by comparing elements, we can guide our search through sorted arrays efficiently. For example, if a < b, we know a cannot match any element in the other array that is b or larger, so we advance the pointer in the first array. This reduces unnecessary checks. In code, comparisons are typically done using operators like <, >, ==, returning boolean values. This operation is foundational because it underpins sorting algorithms (which rely on comparisons to order elements) and the two-pointer logic.

connectsTo.reason: Comparisons are used within sorting algorithms

timeComplexity.note: per comparison, and space is O(1)

commonMistakes: using incorrect operators (e.g
<= instead of < when handling duplicates) or not considering edge cases like negative numbers
but standard comparisons handle these correctly. To avoid errors
ensure comparisons are consistent with the algorithm's needs.  Connection to L5: It builds on the concept of numbers
order
explanation.codePattern: if a < b: # handle less than; elif a == b: # handle equal; else: # handle greater than

Step 1: Compare -2 and -2. Step 2: Evaluate -2 < -2: false. Step 3: Evaluate -2 == -2: true. Step 4: Therefore, a equals b.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Numbers and Order
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: grasping numeric values and their relative positions.

explanation.prose: This foundation involves the basic understanding that numbers have values and can be ordered from smallest to largest (or vice versa). In programming, numbers are represented as integers, floats, etc., and we can compare them to determine their sequence. For the intersection problem, this concept is essential because we need to sort arrays and compare elements to find matches. The intuition is that order allows us to organize data systematically: for example, knowing that 3 is greater than 2 helps us arrange [2, 3] in ascending order. This is a prerequisite for all higher levels, as without it, sorting and comparisons would not make sense. In practice, this involves recognizing numeric literals (e.g., 1, -5, 3.14) and understanding operations like less than (<), greater than (>), and equals (==). Time and space complexity are not applicable here as it's a conceptual foundation.

connectsTo.reason: This concept enables the basic operation of comparing numbers

commonMistakes: confusing order for negative numbers or decimals
but standard number systems handle these consistently. To build this foundation
practice ordering small lists of numbers manually

approaches.name: Hash Set O(n+m)
approaches.summary: Store elements of the first array in a hash set, then iterate through the second array to check for presence in the set. This provides fast lookups and handles duplicates if needed.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the set, plus O(k) for result

ladders.title: Full Problem — Intersection of Two Arrays Using Hash Set
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find common elements between two arrays using hash set approach

explanation.prose: The full problem requires finding all elements that appear in both input arrays. The hash set approach provides an optimal O(n+m) time solution by leveraging fast O(1) lookups. The intuition is to convert the first array into a set for instant membership testing, then iterate through the second array to identify common elements. This approach naturally handles duplicates by using a set for the result. The algorithm begins by creating a hash set from the first array, which eliminates duplicates and enables O(1) lookups. Then we iterate through the second array, checking if each element exists in the set. When a match is found, we add it to a result set to avoid duplicates in the output. Finally, we convert the result set to a list. The .

connectsTo.reason: by implementing the complete intersection logic using the key subroutine of set creation and lookup

timeComplexity.note: where n and m are array lengths, as we traverse each array once with constant-time operations

spaceComplexity.note: where n is the size of the first set and k is the number of unique intersections

commonMistakes: not handling duplicates properly (returning duplicates in output)
forgetting to convert sets to lists
not considering empty array cases

  - 7 in {5}: NO
Final result: [5]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Fast Membership Testing with Hash Sets
ladders.desc: Using hash sets to check if elements exist in a collection in O(1) time

explanation.prose: This core logic demonstrates why hash sets enable efficient intersection algorithms. The intuition is that hash tables provide average O(1) time for membership testing ('in' checks), compared to O(n) for lists or O(log n) for sorted arrays. A hash set works by storing elements in buckets based on their hash values, allowing direct access without scanning the entire collection. The algorithm involves: 1) Adding elements to a hash set using the add() operation, which computes a hash and places the element in the appropriate bucket. 2) Checking membership using the 'in' operator, which computes the same hash and checks the corresponding bucket. The time complexity for both add and 'in' operations is O(1) average case, though worst-case could be O(n) with many collisions.

connectsTo.reason: by providing the fundamental technique that makes the sub-routine efficient

commonMistakes: not understanding that hash sets don't maintain order
confusing average-case with worst-case complexity
not realizing that hashability requirements mean some objects can't be stored in sets

examples.input: Add elements [1,2,3] to set, then check [2,4]
Add elements [5,5,5] to set, then check [5,6]
